elements.sent-2411-001: The Herglotz representation theorem for holomorphic functions with non-negative real part is a fundamental result in the theory of holomorphic functions.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we reinterpret the Herglotz representation in the context of modern techniques, specifically realization formula.
elements.sent-2411-003: This reinterpretation is then extended to operator-valued functions on arbitrary sets, in association with a collection of test functions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Given a regular Borel measure $\mu$ on the unit circle $\mathbb{T}$, the function \begin{equation}f(z)= i Imf(0) + \int_{\mathbb T} \frac{\alpha+z}{\alpha-z}d\mu(\alpha) \label{Her}\\\end{equation} is holomorphic on the open unit disc $\mathbb{D}$ with a non-negative real part.
elements.sent-2411-005: Interestingly, any holomorphic function on $\mathbb{D}$ having non-negative real part can be uniquely expressed through a positive regular Borel measure on $\mathbb{T}$ as depicted in equation \eqref{Her .}
elements.sent-2411-006: This fundamental result is attributed to Herglotz \cite{Her .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, if we assume the normalizing condition $f(0)=1$, the measure $\mu$ corresponds to a regular Borel probability measure on $\mathbb{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Given a domain $\Omega$ in $\mathbb C^d$ and a Hilbert space $\mathcal E$, the Herglotz class $\mathfrak H(\Omega, \mathcal B (\mathcal E))$ consists of all $\mathcal B(\mathcal E)$-valued holomorphic functions on $\Omega$ which have non-negative real parts.
elements.sent-2411-009: It is natural to inquire whether we can generalize the Herglotz representation for operator-valued functions on arbitrary domains.
elements.sent-2411-010: Achieving such a integral represenation on any general domain is typically challenging.
elements.sent-2411-011: The main difficulty is to have suitable integrand for the representation.
elements.sent-2411-012: Nevertheless, a version of Herglotz's integral representation is known for multi-connected domains \cite{AHR, BH, KH , the polydisc \cite{Kor-Puk , the Euclidean unit ball \cite{Kor-Puk, Jury, MP , and domains possessing certain convexity properties \cite{AZ where the integrand is concocted with the reproducing kernel of the Hardy Hilbert spaces of these domains.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this note, the Herglotz representation is extended for a class of functions on an arbitrary set by employing the theory of operators on Hilbert spaces, subject to certain conditions on the set.
elements.sent-2411-014: The power of Herglotz's theorem is well-known - see for example \cite{DM where the Herglotz representation has been crucially used in showing failure of rational dilation on a triply connected planar domain or \cite{AHR for a proof of the von Neumann inequality using it.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Herglotz’s theorem has applications to de Branges-Rovnyak spaces (see the seminal work \cite{Sar ).}
elements.sent-2411-016: Also, it has applications to two-phase composite materials, where the scalar-valued Herglotz functions correspond to the effective properties of the composite materials and the operator-valued Herglotz functions are applied to study the permeability tensor of a porous material, see \cite{OL .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recently, the Carath\'eodory approximation result was proven in \cite{BBK using Herglotz's theorem.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Note that the equation \eqref{Her connects with the theory of operators on a Hilbert space in an obvious way.}
elements.sent-2411-019: By considering $L^2(\mathbb{T , \mu)$ and the normal operator $U$ (with spectrum contained in the unit circle and hence a unitary operator in this case) of multiplication by the co-ordinate $z$, the equation above takes the form \begin{equation}f(z) = i Imf(0) + (\langle (U + z)(U - z)^{-1} 1 , 1 \rangle) Ref(0) \label{RF}\end{equation} where $1$ is the constant function $1$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Conversely, for any unitary operator $U$ on some Hilbert space $\cM$ and a unit vector $\xi$ the function \begin{equation}f(z) = i Imf(0) + \langle (U + z)(U - z)^{-1} \xi , \xi\rangle_{\cM} Ref(0)\end{equation} is in the Herglotz class.
elements.sent-2411-021: We shall call \eqref{RF a {\em realization formula for the Herglotz class functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The realization formula \eqref{RF has been extended to the bidisc by Agler, Tully-Doyle, and Young \cite{Agler, AY-Book .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Additionally, Ball and Kaliuzhnyi-Verbovetskyi \cite{Ball-V have generalized it for a subclass of $\mathfrak H(\mathbb{D ^d)$, including extensions to operator-valued functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, it has been explored in non-commutative setups \cite{PPD .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this note, by leveraging the realization formula on a domain associated with the test functions which we will discuss this in more detail in Section \ref{Re , along with Krein space theory and the Kolmogorov decomposition, we extend the Herglotz representation \eqref{RF to arbitrary domains associated with the test functions, which is a more modern theme now.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The main theorem of this paper is the following: \begin{thm Let $\Psi$ be a collection of test functions on a set $X$ such that there exists a point $x_0\in X$ with $\psi(x_0)=0$ for all $\psi\in \Psi$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a Hilbert space and $f:X\rightarrow \mathcal{B (\mathcal{E )$ be a function.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, some particular cases of \cref{Main as well as examples will be provided in the last section.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we extend our heartfelt thanks to Prof.
elements.sent-2411-032: Tirthankar Bhattacharyya for valuable discussions and insights.
elements.section-2411-002: The Realization Formula and The Kolmogorov Decomposition
elements.sent-2411-033: A remarkable result in function-theoretic operator theory is the realization formula, which asserts that any holomorphic function \(\varphi: \mathbb{D \rightarrow \overline{\mathbb{D }\) can be represented as \begin{equation}\varphi(z)=A +zB(I-zD)^{-1}C\end{equation} for an isometry $ U= \left[\begin{smallmatrix A B C D \end{smallmatrix}\right]$ on $\mathbb{C}\oplus\cH$, where $\cH$ is a Hilbert space.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: A holomorphic function $\theta$ on a domain $\Omega \subseteq \mathbb{C ^d$, taking values in $\cB(\cE)$, the $C^*$-algebra of bounded linear operators on some Hilbert space $\cE$, is called a {\em Schur class function if $\|\theta(z) \| \leq 1$ for all $z$ in $\Omega$.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The realization formula for Schur class functions has been extended to various domains such as an annulus \cite{DM , the bidisc \cite{AM-Book , the complex unit ball \cite{Ball-Bolo , and the symmetrized bidisc \cite{SYM_Real, Tirtha-Hari-JFA .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Notably, not every Schur class function on the polydisc $\mathbb{D ^d$ ($d\geq 3$) possesses a realization formula.}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, a specific subclass, known as the {\em Schur-Agler class , of the Schur class on $\mathbb{D ^d$ does; see \cite{Agler .}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: This concept has further been generalized to an abstract setting where the domain $\Omega$ is replaced by a set $X$, and the Schur class is replaced by a particular class of functions depending on a collection of test functions $\Psi$ defined on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-039: This class is referred to as the {\em $\Psi$-Schur-Agler class .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, explore recent interesting work on this class of functions in the operator-valued setting \cite{BBD, BPT, CHL, Hartz, K2, K3, KRT .}
elements.sent-2411-041: A collection $\Psi$ of $\bC$-valued functions on a set $X$ is called a set of {\em test functions (see \cite{DM ) if the following conditions hold:.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The collection $\Psi$ inherits a subspace topology of the space of all bounded functions from $X$ to $\overline{\mathbb D}$ endowed with topology of pointwise convergence.
elements.sent-2411-043: We shall denote the algebra of bounded continuous functions over $\Psi$ with pointwise algebra operation by $C_b(\Psi)$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Define an injective mapping $E:X\rightarrow C_b(\Psi)$ as $E(x)= e_x$, where $e_x(\psi)=\psi(x).$.
elements.sent-2411-046: A function $k: X\times X \rightarrow \mathcal{B (\cE)$ is said to be a $\mathcal{B (\cE)$-valued positive kernel if for any $n \geq 1$, any $n$ points $x_1, \dots, x_n$ in $X$ and any $n$ vectors $e_1, \dots, e_n$ in $\cE$ we have \begin{equation}\sum_{i,j=1}^n \langle k(x_i, x_j)e_j, e_i \rangle \geq 0 .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: We define a map $\Lambda: X \times X \to \mathcal{B (C_b(\Psi), \mathcal{B (\cE))$ to be a completely positive kernel if \begin{equation}\sum_{i,j=1}^n T_i^* \Lambda(x_i, x_j)\left(\overline{f_i}f_j \right)T_j\geq0\end{equation}for any $n\geq 1$, any $n$ points $x_1, \dots, x_n\in X $, any $n$ operators $T_1, \dots, T_n $ in $\cB(\cE)$ and any $n$ functions $g_1, \dots, g_n$ in $C_b(\Psi)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Now, we shall state the famous Kolmogorov decomposition of a completely positive kernel which is one of the key ingredients for the proof of our main result.
elements.sent-2411-049: Then, $\Lambda: X\times X\rightarrow\mathcal{B}\left(C_b(\Psi), \cB(\cE)\right)$ is a completely positive kernel if and only if there exist an auxiliary Hilbert space $\cK$, a map $h: X \rightarrow \cB(\cK, \cE)$ and a unital $*$-representation $\rho: C_b(\Psi)\rightarrow \cB(\cK)$ such that \begin{equation}\Lambda(x, y)(g)= h(x) \rho(g) h(y)^*\end{equation} for all $x, y\in X$ and $g\in C_b(\Psi)$.
elements.sent-2411-050: We say that $f: X \rightarrow \cB(\cE)$ is in $H^\infty_{\Psi (\cE)$ if there is a non-negative constant $C$ such that the function \begin{equation}\label{HH} (x, y)\mapsto\left(C^2 k(x,y) - f(x)k(x,y) f(y)^* \right)\end{equation} is a $\cB(\cE)$-valued positive kernel for each $k$ in $\mathcal{K _{\Psi (\cE)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: If $f$ is in $H^\infty_{\Psi (\cE)$, then we denote by $C_f$ the smallest $C$ which satisfies \eqref{HH .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The collection of maps $f\in H^\infty_{\Psi (\cE)$ for which $C_f$ is no larger than $1$ is called the {\em $\Psi$-Schur-Agler class and it is denoted by $\cS\cA_{\Psi (\cE).$ We shall use the following realization formula for operator-valued $\Psi$-Schur-Agler class functions \cite{Tirtha-Ani-Vik, DM .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: A function $f: X \to \cB(\cE)$ is said to be $\Psi$-Herglotz-Agler class function if \begin{equation}(x,y) \mapsto f(x)k(x, y) + k(x, y) f(y)^*\end{equation} is a $\cB(\cE)$-valued positive kernel for each $k$ in $\mathcal{K _{\Psi (\cE)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The realization formula is extremely powerful and leads to numerous significant results.
elements.sent-2411-055: For instance, it enables the derivation of the Pick-Nevanlinna interpolation theorem \cite{AM-Book , proves the commutant lifting theorem \cite{Ball , and establishes the Carath\' eodory approximation result \cite{ABJK .}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: It also aids in the factorization of Schur-Aglar class functions \cite{BK, DJ .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Additionally, its applications extend to fields such as signal processing \cite{Gohberg and electrical engineering \cite{Helton .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In the following section, we will use it to prove \cref{Main .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Now, we are all set to prove the the realization of operator-valued Herglotz functions on a set $X$ i.e., \cref{Main .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The proof is mainly motivated from \cite{Ball-V .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Also, one can explore \cite{Ball-V2 as well as \cite{Ball-IEOT for insights into the application of Krein space theory in the realization formula.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Proof of Theorem \ref{Main: We establish the equivalence of $[H], [D]$ and $[R]$ by showing that \begin{equation}[H]\implies [D] \implies [R]\implies [D]\implies [H].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-064: By Theorem \ref{Thm , there exists a completely positive kernel $\Gamma: X\times X \rightarrow \cB\left(C_b(\Psi), \cB(\cE) \right)$ such that \begin{equation}I-T(x)T(y)^*= \Gamma(x, y)\left(1- E(x)\overline{E(y)} \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-065: After putting the expression of $T$ we get \begin{equation}I- \left(I+f(x) \right)^{-1} \left(I-f(x) \right) \left(I- f(y)^* \right) \left(I+f(y)^* \right)^{-1} =\Gamma(x, y)\left(1- E(x)\overline{E(y)}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-066: A simple calculations yeilds the following \begin{equation}\label{EqD} 2\left(I+ f(x)\right)^{-1} [f(x)+ f(y)^*] \left(I+f(y)^*\right)^{-1}=\Gamma(x, y)\left(1- E(x) \overline{E(y)} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-067: Set \begin{equation}\Lambda(x, y) (g) = \left[\frac{I+ f(x)}{\sqrt{2}}\right]\Gamma(x, y)(g)\left[\frac{I+ f(y)^*}{\sqrt{2}}\right]\end{equation} for $g\in C_b(\Psi)$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Then from equation \eqref{EqD , we have \begin{equation}f(x)+ f(y)^* = \Lambda (x,y) \left(1- E(x)\overline{E(y)} \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-069: If we show that $\Lambda(x, y)$ is completely positive kernel, then we are done.
elements.sent-2411-070: This is readily observed by applying Kolmogorov decomposition (Theorem \ref{KD ) to the completely positive kernel $\Gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Indeed, if $\Gamma(x,y)(g)= h(x)\rho(g)h(y)^*$ is a Kolmogorov decomposition of $\Gamma$ where $\rho$ is a unital $*$-representation of $C_b(\Psi)$, then from the definition of $\Lambda$ in terms of $\Gamma$, it is easy to read off that \begin{equation}\Lambda(x,y)(g)= H(x)\rho(g) H(y)^*\end{equation} where $H(x)= \frac{1 {\sqrt{2 (I+f(x))h(x)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Thus $\Lambda$ has Kolmogorov decomposition and hence it is a completely positive kernel.
elements.sent-2411-073: Then by Theorem \ref{KD , there exist a Hilbert space $\cK$ and a unital $*$-homomorphism $\rho : C_b(\Psi) \to \cB(\cK)$ and a function $h: X \to \cB(\cK, \cE)$ such that $\Lambda(x,y)(g)= h(x)\rho(g)h(y)^*$ for $g\in C_b(\Psi)$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: This can be rewritten as \begin{equation}\label{lurking-eq} f(x)+f(y)^* + h(x) \rho(E(x)) \rho(E(y))^*h(y)^* = h(x) h(y)^*.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: Now, we shall employ the Krein-space theory, see \cite{Krein-Bog for more details.}
